steps.prompt: Which word is the main finite verb?

hints.deficere: 'Deficere' is an infinitive meaning 'to fail/lose heart'

steps.target: to be necessary / it is fitting
choices: to be necessary / it is fitting

hints.to open: 'To open' is 'aperire' — 'Oportet' comes from 'oportere' (it is necessary)
hints.to work: 'To work' is 'operari' or 'laborare' — 'oportere' means 'it is necessary'

steps.reveal: Oportet = it is necessary / one ought

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'oporteat' — 'Oportet' is indicative
hints.imperative: 'Oportet' is impersonal — impersonal verbs do not have imperatives in standard usage

hints.aperio, aperire, aperui, apertum: These are the parts of 'aperire' (to open)
hints.operor, operari, operatus sum, —: These are the parts of 'operari' (to work) — a deponent
hints.opto, optare, optavi, optatum: These are the parts of 'optare' (to choose/wish)

steps.target: it has no personal subject — the infinitive phrase serves as its subject
choices: it has no personal subject — the infinitive phrase serves as its subject

hints.it is always passive: 'Oportet' is active in form — it is impersonal because it lacks a personal subject
hints.it can only be 1st person: 'Oportet' is 3rd person singular — impersonal means it uses only the 3rd sg. form
hints.it has no conjugated forms: 'Oportet' does conjugate (oportet, oportebat, oportuit) — it just lacks a personal subject

hints.gerund: The gerund would be 'orandi/orandum' — 'orare' is the infinitive
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'oret' — 'orare' is infinitive

steps.reveal: deficere = to fail / to lose heart

hints.imperative: Imperative would be 'defice' — 'deficere' is the infinitive
hints.perfect infinitive: Perfect infinitive would be 'defecisse' — 'deficere' is present
hints.gerundive: A gerundive would be 'deficiendum' — 'deficere' is the infinitive

hints.conjunction: A conjunction joins clauses — 'semper' modifies the verb 'orare' as 'always'
hints.adjective: An adjective would modify a noun — 'semper' modifies the verb
hints.preposition: A preposition would govern a case — 'semper' is an adverb

translation: It is necessary to pray always and not to lose heart
